channel.title: Major Digest - Reliable and Comprehensive News Sources: tech
channel.description: Get the latest news online at Major Digest, the top online news resource for the most reliable news sources. Stay informed about the world around you!

image.title: Major Digest - Reliable and Comprehensive News Sources: tech

item.title: Unannounced 32TB Seagate hard drive surfaces in Japan for photoshoot
item.description: Seagate’s unannounced 32TB IronWolf Pro hard drive surfaced in Japanese retail, marking a quiet shift of extreme capacity toward consumers.
item.dc:creator: Efosa Udinmwen, TechRadar

item.title: Grok makes sexual images of kids as users test AI guardrails
item.description: Elon Musk's artificial intelligence chatbot Grok created sexualized images of minors on the social media platform X in response to user prompts in recent days, drawing criticism of a tool that positions itself as less restrained than its competition.
item.dc:creator: Bloomberg, The Mercury News

item.title: One UI 8.5 leaks show off Samsung's new Privacy Display feature for phones, and Bixby powered by Perplexity AI
item.description: A major software update for Samsung phones is on the way, and we just got a preview of two of its new features.
item.dc:creator: David Nield, TechRadar

item.title: US cybersecurity experts plead guilty to attacking US companies with ransomware
item.description: Two cybersecurity professionals charged with running a ransomware operation have pleaded guilty to conspiring to obstruct, delay, or affect commerce through extortion. They will be sentenced on March 12, 2026, the US Department of Justice announced this week.
item.dc:creator: Computer World

item.title: Opinion: EPA helping AI industry grow but ignoring technology’s power to protect environment
item.description: Agency must use artificial intelligence to defend communities with same zeal it applies to cutting protections that safeguard them.
item.dc:creator: Avi Garbow, The Mercury News

item.title: The Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 and Z Flip 8 could be lighter than ever
item.description: A new year means new foldables, and we might have learned some more about Samsung's next foldable phones.
item.dc:creator: David Nield, TechRadar

item.title: European banks may lay off 200,000 due to AI
item.description: Over 200,000 banking jobs in Europe could disappear by 2030 as banks invest more in AI and close physical branches according to a new analysis by Morgan Stanley reported by the Financial Times. This is equivalent to around 10% of the workforce at 35 major banks.
item.dc:creator: Computer World

item.title: Deepseek says new method can train AI more efficiently and cheaply
item.description: Chinese AI company Deepseek has unveiled a new training method, Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections (mHC), which will make it possible to train large language models more efficiently and at lower cost, reports the South China Morning Post.
item.dc:creator: Computer World

item.title: For Apple in ’26, it’s time to take the ‘Snow Leopard’ approach to OS updates
item.description: If the current speculation is correct, Apple’s 2026 operating system releases will follow a familiar path: they’re more likely to mimic the macOS Snow Leopard pattern set in 2009 than deliver a raft of snazzy new features.
item.dc:creator: Computer World

item.title: China requires chipmakers to use at least 50% domestic equipment
item.description: According to Reuters, China is now requiring its chipmakers to use at least 50% domestically made equipment when expanding their production capacity. The rule is not publicly documented, but chipmakers must show that at least half of their equipment is Chinese to get government approval.
item.dc:creator: Network World

item.title: The top 10 AI stories of 2025
item.description: It’s safe to say that AI was the story of 2025: It topped CIOs’ tech agendas; vendors rolled out a constant stream of new AI products and features; and IT professionals and other knowledge workers got down to the business of integrating it into their workflows.
item.dc:creator: CIO

item.title: Digital surveys may have hit the AI point of no return
item.description: There’s bad news for those using digital surveys to try to understand people’s online behavior: We may no longer be able to determine whether a human is responding to them or not, a recent study has shown—and there seems to be no way around this problem.
item.dc:creator: S.A. Applin, Fast Company
